facts.text: Italy is a southern European country shaped like a boot extending into the Mediterranean Sea.
Rome is Italy's capital and was once the center of the Roman Empire.
Italy played a central role in the Renaissance, producing figures like Leonardo da Vinci, Michelangelo, and Raphael.
Venice is famous for its canals, gondolas, and long history as a maritime republic.
Florence is often called the cradle of the Renaissance.
The Vatican City, the world's smallest independent state, is located within Rome.
Italian cuisine includes pasta, pizza, risotto, gelato, espresso, and highly regional specialties.
The Alps form Italy's northern mountain border, while the Apennines run down much of the peninsula.
Italy is home to famous landmarks such as the Colosseum, Leaning Tower of Pisa, Pompeii, and the Amalfi Coast.
Mount Vesuvius, the volcano that buried Pompeii in AD 79, still towers near Naples.
Italian art, fashion, opera, cinema, and design have had global influence for centuries.
Milan is one of the world's major fashion capitals.
Italy has more UNESCO World Heritage Sites than almost any other country.
Modern Italy unifies deep ancient history with contemporary culture, sports, and tourism.
Rome is Italy's capital and one of the most historically influential cities in the world.
Its ruins, churches, piazzas, and public art made it a defining center of European history.
Rome is one of the most recognizable places or topics associated with Italy.
Travel guides about Italy frequently feature Rome.
Learning about Rome helps explain part of the geography, culture, or history of Italy.
For many visitors, Rome stands out as a major highlight of Italy.

The story of Rome is closely tied to how many people understand Italy.
